Islamic Tradition

Avicenna (Ibn Sīnā)

c. 980 – 1037

The floating man and the self-aware intellect: one of the great arguments about the soul.

A serious reconstruction of Avicenna’s philosophy of mind has to begin from a methodological point. One cannot isolate the chapters on the human soul from the rest of his system. Avicenna places psychology within natural philosophy as the science of the soul in living bodies, but he also makes psychology depend on metaphysical causes: the Agent Intellect, the celestial souls and intelligences, and the First Principle. In his own architecture, metaphysics does not distract from psychology; it completes it. So a report that treated God, angels, or celestial minds as detachable theological ornament would miss the explanatory structure Avicenna himself built.

What emerges from that wider reading is a layered philosophy of mind. Perception, imagination, estimation, memory, emotion, and much practical cognition are embodied and organ-dependent; the rational soul is nonetheless an immaterial substance; self-awareness is immediate and not reducible to bodily awareness; universal thought depends on contact with the Agent Intellect; animal minds possess genuine but lower-grade awareness; and divine and celestial minds set the formal and causal horizon within which human minds operate. Avicenna’s “philosophy of mind,” in other words, is simultaneously psychology, epistemology, cosmology, and philosophical theology.

Avicenna also does not use one single term equivalent to the modern English word mind. The relevant vocabulary includes nafs (soul/self), quwā (powers or faculties), and ʿaql (intellect). The “science of the soul” belongs to the study of living bodies, but its highest operations cannot be understood without the separate intelligences and the First Principle. That is why the right procedure is not to force him into a modern brain-only model, nor to push his supra-human noetics aside as “merely theological.”

One terminological caution is essential. Avicenna can call the human practical faculty an “active” power, the power that judges good and bad in particulars and governs conduct. He also speaks of the separate Agent Intellect, the last celestial intelligence, as the source of intelligibles and the giver of forms. A careful reconstruction has to keep these apart: one is a power within the human soul, the other is a separate intelligence above the human soul.

The soul as completion and substance

At the natural-philosophical level, Avicenna defines the soul as the first completion or perfection of a natural organic body. He prefers “completion” to “form” or “power” because it captures the soul’s role in making a living body actually alive while remaining broad enough to include cases not exhausted by material embodiment; in one formulation, the term extends even to souls separated from matter. He also stresses that the soul is named in relation to its rule over body, which is why its study initially belongs to natural philosophy.

But this starting point does not yet settle what the soul is metaphysically. Avicenna explicitly warns that calling soul a completion does not by itself prove that it is a substance. He therefore adds further arguments that the rational soul is not a body and not a form impressed in body: intelligible concepts do not fit in a bodily substratum; the intellect, unlike the senses, can know itself; and its activity is not directly exhausted by the deterioration of the organs, even though damaged senses can indirectly impede it. The result is a doctrine in which the rational soul is naturally united to body without being reducible to it.

A good modern summary is that Avicenna is neither a reductive physicalist nor a simple anti-Aristotelian dualist. Rahman’s formulation is still useful: Avicenna begins from Aristotle but gives the soul something like the independence of a pilot relative to a ship, then argues for its substantial separability. Natural philosophy provides the functional and physiological account; metaphysics secures the soul’s immaterial status and its place in the cosmic order.

Embodied mental architecture

For Avicenna, a human being does not literally contain several souls stacked inside one body. Rather, one soul has multiple powers: vegetative powers for nutrition, growth, and reproduction; animal powers for perception, appetite, and locomotion; and rational powers for universal thought and deliberate action. He says plainly that in humans the plant and animal functions are powers of one soul, not separate entities. This matters because it makes the human subject the single bearer of sensing, imagining, desiring, thinking, and acting, even though these operations are sharply differentiated.

Avicenna also gives this hierarchy an orientation. Lower powers serve higher ones, and the highest forms of intellective activity stand at the summit of the human psychic order. In one schematic presentation, the acquired or “adapted” intellect is the terminus toward which the lower faculties are ordered. So the Avicennian mind is not a flat bundle of capacities; it is a graded organization whose end is intellective completion.

His most influential contribution to philosophy of mind is the theory of the internal senses. Beyond the five external senses, Avicenna posits five internal faculties. Common sense integrates the deliverances of the external senses into one percept. The imaginative storehouse retains images after the sensible object is gone. The compositive imagination recombines and separates those images, making fantasy and recomposition possible. Estimation (wahm) grasps non-sensible meanings or intentions in particulars, such as danger, hostility, usefulness, or significance. A second memory retains these intentions, not merely sensible forms. This is an extraordinarily elaborate model of perceptual integration, imagery, nonconceptual appraisal, and memory.

Avicenna treats these faculties as embodied. He localizes them in brain regions and insists that the actions of the internal sensory powers occur through bodily organs, even though his broader physiology still gives the heart priority as the first principle delegating powers to the brain. So below the level of pure intellect, his psychology is strongly embodied: large portions of ordinary mentality are literally organ-based operations.

In contemporary terms, one might say that common sense functions like multisensory binding, the image store like a perceptual buffer, compositive imagination like offline simulation, estimation like rapid appraisal of what a thing means for this creature here and now, and the memory of intentions like retention of non-sensible evaluative content. Avicenna himself would not use those labels, but the structural affinities are genuine and explain why later readers found his model unusually powerful.

His account of imagination is equally important because it is also a theory of error. In sleep, terror, epilepsy, and related states, imagination can present inner forms as if they were external perceptions. Avicenna explicitly describes cases where what comes from within appears to come from outside. Estimation and intellect also help distinguish real percepts from dreams by grasping whether what is perceived has external existence. So he has a surprisingly sophisticated account of hallucination, dreaming, and nonveridical representation.

At the same time, not all dreams are mere bodily overflow. Avicenna allows that some dreams and visions derive from the heavenly order: when bodily and psychic obstacles fall away, imagination can receive information from higher causes. The distinction is therefore not simply between “natural” and “supernatural,” but between lower bodily noise, uncontrolled imagination, and authentic reception from the celestial order.

Intentionality, affect, and practical reason

If one wants the single most original Avicennian contribution to philosophy of mind, estimation is a strong candidate. Avicenna distinguishes the perception of form from the perception of intention: the form is first conveyed by external sense, whereas the intention is grasped by the soul from the sensible thing without first being given as such by external sense. That is why a sheep can perceive a wolf as dangerous, and why a person can judge from a yellow appearance that a thing is honey and sweet even before tasting it. Wahm thus handles a class of contents that are neither bare sensory qualities nor universal concepts.

That faculty is also bound up with affect and particularity. Black emphasizes that estimation stores and retrieves intentions, judges whether a perceived object really exists externally, and functions as a kind of internal judge or “vizier” in the animal soul. It sits near the center of what we would now call nonconceptual intentionality: the grasp of this thing as threatening, useful, attractive, or to-be-fled before conceptual abstraction begins.

Avicenna’s theory of action builds directly on this architecture. Appetite divides roughly into desire for the pleasant and irascible resistance to the harmful. The decisive power moves the organs only after a judgment has marked something as to be pursued or avoided. In humans, practical cognition applies general propositions to particular future situations; in animals, motion follows estimation, desire, and arousal without the mediation of universal thought. This is an early form of practical-syllogistic psychology.

This practical side of mind is also social. Avicenna says that human life essentially requires society, speech, and learned norms of what is permitted and forbidden. The practical intellect is therefore not an isolated calculator; it is the faculty through which universals bear on shared life, ethics, and governance. That point becomes crucial again when he turns to prophecy and lawgiving.

Intellect, knowledge, and self-awareness

At the rational level, Avicenna distinguishes practical from theoretical intellect, and within theoretical intellect he describes several stages of perfection. The material or potential intellect is sheer receptivity. The intellect in habitu possesses first principles and can think when it investigates. The actual intellect can think what it already knows whenever it wishes. The acquired or adapted intellect is the state of full present intellection. Above ordinary human stages stands the holy intellect, an exceptional aptitude for immediate reception.

Between stepwise syllogistic thought and prophetic immediacy, Avicenna also recognizes mushāhada, a kind of direct vision or habituated acquaintance with intelligibles. Here the mind no longer traverses middle terms discursively, yet the syllogistic structure remains present. So even his notion of intuitive cognition is not anti-rational; it is rationality intensified and temporally compressed.

He also attributes to the mind an unusual form of pre-articulate possession: simple knowledge. A person may be certain that he knows how to answer a question before he has yet unfolded the answer discursively. Rahman notes that Avicenna treats this as a higher, creative form of knowing and even compares it to divine knowledge, whereas discursive human reasoning is a lower “psychic” mode. This is one of the clearest places where his philosophy of mind joins epistemology to a hierarchy of cognitive forms.

Still, Avicenna does not deny the empirical beginning of human cognition. He repeatedly says that the senses are the means by which the soul acquires knowledge, and that the human intellect, unlike celestial intellect, must move from perceived effects toward intelligible causes. External and internal senses therefore prepare human thought; without them, the human intellect in this world would not be actualized. In that respect Avicenna is more empiricist than a superficial reading of his emanationism might suggest.

Yet sense data alone never complete the story. Avicenna’s characteristic thesis is that the internal senses and human reasoning prepare the soul, but the intelligible becomes fully actual only through the Agent Intellect. Some summaries make this sound as though universals are simply donated from above; Hasse’s interpretation is more exact. The human soul really does compare and contrast images, abstract essential from accidental features, and make itself ready; but the final actualization of the intelligible still depends on the illumination or mediation of the Agent Intellect. The most faithful reconstruction is therefore not abstraction or emanation, but abstraction through emanative mediation.

This has a striking consequence for memory. Avicenna denies that the immaterial intellect stores universals the way brain-based faculties store images or intentions. What we call remembering a universal is, strictly speaking, renewed contact with the Agent Intellect by a mind already disposed to receive the same intelligible again. Learned knowledge is therefore retained dispositionally rather than as a warehouse of abstract forms sitting inside the intellect. In a modern idiom, Avicenna is committed to a kind of extra-human or external dependence of intellectual memory.

He is also explicit that this “contact” is not mystical automation. The Agent Intellect lets intelligibles flow only in accordance with the soul’s prepared demand; when the soul turns away, the effluence stops. So Avicenna’s noetics is rigorously structured, not a vague appeal to inspiration. Ordinary learning, direct vision of intelligibles, philosophy, and prophecy all share the same architecture, even if they differ dramatically in degree.

No element of Avicenna’s philosophy of mind has attracted more attention than the floating or suspended man. The thought experiment asks us to imagine a human brought into being all at once, deprived of sensory input and of contact with his own body. Avicenna’s claim is that such a subject would still affirm his own existence while not affirming the existence of limbs or external things. Rahman rightly treats this as a proof of the soul’s substantiality; in the De anima tradition Avicenna adds that the “I” is what is designated when one says “I sensed,” “I knew,” or “I acted.”

The deepest point is that self-awareness is immediate. Avicenna says the self is aware of itself without any external thing mediating this awareness, and later interpreters stress that this awareness is direct, intrinsic, and continuous in humans. This is not yet the same as an explicit theory of introspective judgment or higher-order consciousness; it is more primitive. It is the self’s presence to itself.

But Avicenna also says more than that. He argues that the intellect understands itself, understands its instrument, and understands itself to understand; elsewhere he says that when the soul understands itself, it understands itself as itself, as understanding, as intellect, and as understood. So Avicenna either distinguishes, or at least gives us the materials to distinguish, primitive self-awareness from higher self-intellection. The floating man secures the first; the mature rational soul can sometimes achieve the second.

This distinction helps explain personal identity. Hennig argues, drawing on Avicenna’s later texts, that self-awareness is what guarantees continued numerical identity and in a strong sense even constitutes the very existence of the self. Avicenna’s point is that although embodied association initially individuates human souls, ongoing self-awareness can preserve that individuality after bodily separation. That is one reason postmortem personal survival is philosophically possible in his system.

At the same time, Avicenna does not put human self-knowledge on a par with divine self-knowledge. Hennig’s reconstruction is persuasive here: only the Necessary Existent intellects itself with perfect identity of knower and known. The human soul may intellect itself as an individual, but this is an extraordinary achievement and still not the same as the absolutely simple divine self-intellection. The divine mind is the limiting case by which every lower mode of self-knowing is measured.

Animal, celestial, and divine minds

Avicenna’s philosophy of mind is importantly nonhuman. Animals share many of the internal faculties humans have and therefore possess genuine perception, imagination, dream-life, appetite, and even a kind of self-awareness. But their self-awareness is indirect, mixed with perception, intermittent, and body-bound; it is mediated by estimation rather than grounded in an immaterial rational self. They can be aware of themselves as perceiving or acting individuals, but not with the direct, continuous, intrinsically self-present awareness characteristic of rational souls.

This is philosophically rich because it avoids both extremes. Animals are not mindless automata, yet their mentality does not collapse into human rational consciousness. Mind, for Avicenna, is a graded reality: vegetative life, animal perception and estimation, human intellect, celestial souls, celestial intelligences, and divine intellect form a hierarchy rather than a flat divide between minded and non-minded beings.

Here the supra-human levels become indispensable. In Avicenna’s metaphysics, the First Principle is itself intelligence, intellecting subject, and intelligible object. From its timeless self-intellection there proceeds the first intelligence, and from the complex self-thinking of created intelligences there proceeds the rest of the celestial order: further intelligences, souls, and heavenly bodies. Avicenna explicitly notes that these celestial entities are sometimes called angels in religious terms. So “angelic mind” in Avicenna is not decorative piety; it names real levels within his ontology of cognition.

The divine mind is unique. God knows all things through knowing Himself; multiplicity is known by the First as a whole and at once, not by discursive progression. Avicenna also interprets divine intellection as inseparable from will and love: the First comprehends, intends, wills, and loves its own intellection, and the procession of being follows from this atemporal act. Human and celestial intellects are congeneric with one another; the First is not merely the highest member of that class, but the unique Necessary Existent.

Below God, Avicenna distinguishes celestial intelligences from celestial souls. The intelligences are utterly immaterial and their cognition is universal. The celestial souls are bound to celestial bodies and, precisely because of that psychic-corporeal relation, can know particulars and determine the particular motions of the spheres. Marmura emphasizes that this is why celestial souls, rather than celestial intelligences, can transmit particular images and future-oriented information to prophets through imagination. In the psychology of prophecy, this distinction is decisive.

Avicenna also insists that human and celestial intellects are congeneric immaterial substances. The difference is not kind but condition: celestial intellects are always actual and know from causes, whereas human intellect begins in potency and must ascend from sense and imagination. That is why human philosophy is both possible and limited. The human mind can become like the celestial order, but only by a long process of preparation and actualization.

At the lowest celestial level stands the Agent Intellect, the last intelligence, also called the giver or bestower of forms. It is the founding principle of human intellection and of form-giving in the sublunary world. Put bluntly: if one removes the Agent Intellect from Avicenna’s psychology, one has not purified his philosophy of mind; one has removed the mechanism by which human universal thought occurs.

Prophecy, identity, and afterlife

Prophecy is therefore the apex, not the embarrassment, of Avicenna’s philosophy of mind. He treats it as the highest realization of the same psychic architecture that underlies ordinary cognition. There are at least two main revelatory channels: intellectual revelation, in which the rational faculty receives abstract intelligibles from the Agent Intellect, and imaginative revelation, in which the imaginative faculty receives particular images from celestial souls. Classical summaries of Avicenna add a practical-political dimension as well, since the prophet is also lawgiver and founder.

What distinguishes the prophet from the philosopher is not a different kind of mind, but an extraordinary perfection of the same kind. Avicenna says the prophet receives intelligibles all at once or nearly so, with middle terms included, by means of the holy intellect. At the same time, a perfected imagination translates this universal knowledge into speech, images, symbols, and scriptural form that ordinary people can grasp. Some summaries even note that Avicenna can call the Agent Intellect the Holy Spirit. Prophecy, then, is not irrational eruption; it is peak cognition: immediate intellect, perfected imagination, and practical authority integrated in one soul.

He also attributes unusual causal power to the prophetic soul. In some discussions, a noble and strong soul can influence matter, heal, restrain vicious people, or bring about extraordinary effects, and Avicenna links such capacities to prophecy rather than to arbitrary magic. Whatever one thinks of those claims, they show that for him mind can be causally efficacious beyond its own body at the highest levels of perfection.

Avicenna’s theory of personal survival follows naturally from this framework. He denies transmigration because each soul is individuated in association with a particular body and with the experiences it undergoes through that body. Souls do not pre-exist as a plurality waiting to enter bodies; they come to be when a body is prepared for them, and their individuality begins there. Yet once individuated, they can remain many after bodily separation because they have already acquired distinct existence, histories, and self-awareness.

This survival is differentiated by psychic development. All external and internal sensory faculties perish with the body, but the rational soul does not. Souls that have become intellectually perfected achieve a form of paradise consisting in intellective conjunction and delight; souls that remain tied to imagination persist at the imaginative level and experience quasi-bodily pleasures and pains. Avicenna’s eschatology is therefore also a philosophy of mind: after death, what remains depends on which parts of mentality were genuinely immaterial and which were only body-dependent.

The philosophy of mind that emerges

If one now extracts a comprehensive Avicennian philosophy of mind, several core theses emerge. First, mind is functionally layered: vegetative, sensory, imaginative, estimative, appetitive, practical, and theoretical operations are distinct and ordered. Second, much of mind is embodied: perception, imagery, estimation, emotion, and most ordinary memory depend on organs. Third, the rational soul is nevertheless a substance whose highest acts are not reducible to organ function. Fourth, universal thought is neither self-generated by brain processes nor infused arbitrarily, but arises through prepared contact with the Agent Intellect. Fifth, self-awareness is primitive and immediate, while higher self-intellection is reflexive and rare. Sixth, minds exist on a continuum from animals to humans to celestial souls and intelligences, with God as the unique case of pure self-intellective actuality.

In present-day philosophical vocabulary, that makes Avicenna a theorist of embodied cognition below the rational level, of nonreductive separability at the level of intellect, of nonconceptual content through estimation, of primitive first-person consciousness through direct self-awareness, of extra-human dependence in noetics through the Agent Intellect, and of graded mental ontology across animal, human, celestial, and divine beings. One framework covers perception, imagination, appetite, intellect, prophecy, and postmortem survival.

There are also real tensions within the system. One is the exact balance between abstraction and illumination: some texts sound as though universals are simply donated, while others make human comparison and abstraction indispensable. Another is the relation between primitive self-awareness and explicit self-intellection. A third is the status of the soul as both completion/form of a body and a separable substance. A fourth is the delicate distribution of knowledge of particulars across God, celestial intelligences, and celestial souls. These are not reasons to dismiss Avicenna; they are the places where the depth of the system becomes most visible.

One further developmental point is worth noting. Later Avicennian texts seem to sharpen the separation between intellect and the bodily internal senses, pushing intuition farther from any physiological base. That suggests not a rejection of the earlier faculty model, but a growing desire to protect the immateriality and immortality of the rational soul from being absorbed by its bodily instruments.

Final assessment

The most faithful conclusion is that Avicenna offers not merely a theory of the human soul, but a full metaphysics of mind. Its lower levels are physiological and representational; its middle levels are practical, imaginative, and intellectual; its upper levels are celestial and divine. God’s mind and the minds of angels or celestial beings are not detachable theological ornaments. They explain why human intellect can know at all, how prophecy is possible, how the order of intelligibles is grounded, and what the perfected end of mind looks like. The most accurate formula is this: for Avicenna, philosophy of mind is not psychology with theology subtracted, but psychology completed by metaphysics.

The next productive step would be to turn this report into a source-based outline keyed to Avicenna’s primary texts, separating what comes from the De anima proper, what comes from later works such as the Pointers and Notes, and where the main scholarly disputes lie.
